Research-Projects/Pulsed-Magnet-Lab/Cupr.filePath: Research Projects/Pulsed Magnet Lab/Cuprous Oxide Rydberg Excitons/Transmission Spectroscopy for Cuprous Oxide.md
Research-Projects/Pulsed-Magnet-Lab/Cupr.title: Transmission Spectroscopy for Cuprous Oxide
Research-Projects/Pulsed-Magnet-Lab/Cupr.content:
What are the key optical and instrumental parameters required to design and optimise a transmission spectroscopy setup for detecting Rydberg Excitons in Cuprous Oxide?
Of course, we require the following:

High Spectral resolution
Cryogenic (<4K) operation
Highly controlled optical power
High quality single crystal sample with a small enough (how much?) thickness
Strain-free mounting
Precise temperature control

The resolution requirement depends upon the target principal quantum number. I do not know how far down we can go with our monochromator/grating - but it should definitely be less than 100 \mu e V for the Yellow-Series 1. Cryogenic operation, below 4K is also crucial 1.
There is a requirement that the optical power of the exciting laser be carefully controlled for the experiment to be successful. This essentially reduces the population of excitons and can be adjusted so that exciton-exciton interactions can be ignored 2.


                  
                  Relevant paper for absorption spectroscopy of Cuprous Oxide 
                  
                

Link: doi.org/10.1088/1367-2630/ad8c74
Title: Microwave-optical spectroscopy of Rydberg excitons in the ultrastrong driving regime.
Why is it relevant?
The paper describes an experiment where they drive Cuprous Oxide sample into the “Ultrastrong” regime. This essentially translates to a high quantum number target for the Rydberg excitons observed in the experiment.
Discussed more here: Microwave-optical spectroscopy of Rydberg excitons in the ultrastrong driving regine, Brewin et al


Optimal parameters for such optical absorption spectroscopy mentioned in 2 are:

Light source centered around 571-580 nm
Focusing optics of Numerical Aperture 0.42-0.6

Natural single crystals generally outperform synthetic samples, with n=12-25 states being observed in the former 2. In synthetic samples, n=7-10 is normally observable 3.
This is primarily due to lower defect densities and reduced copper vacancy concentrations in the natural samples 3.
4 describes an experiment carried out with a natural crystal that was mounted on a transparent substrate and oriented such that the 111 crystal plane was parallel to the surface. It was mechanically polished to ~ 50 \mu m. A 2x3 mm rectangle of this sample was mounted on a 5 mm diameter CaF2 window. Experimental details are present in 5. Note that strain-free mounting is very important (Prevents SHG generation in bulk due to destruction of inversion symmetry).
Thus, the most important factor in being able to observe higher-n states of excitons is the sample quality rather than the instrumental capability. Above n=20, the electron-hole plasma and charged impurities quench the states 6.
The states here are spaced as n^{-3}, and thus spectral resolution requirements scale with target quantum number. It hits around 10 \mu e V beyond n=20.
Detection accuracy is greatly enhanced by finer temperature control and coherent transfer matrix analysis methods 6. (Read up on this. Coherent Transfer Matrix analysis)


                  
                  Survey of observed states for yellow-series Rydberg excitons in literature 
                  
                

Most experimental studies detected states between n=7 and n=13. Cryogenic temperatures (below 150K) were used in all, with the lowest temperatures used being 1.2K.


Transmission Spectroscopy Instrumental Configurations
Spectrometer and Detection Systems
There were a variety of spectrometer configurations used across the studies reviewed. Mostly, the typical grating monochromators of resolutions between 70 \mu eV - 10 \mu eV were used, with some even surpassing this range.


                  
                  Case Study Spectrometer: Princeton Instruments HRS-750 
                  
                


1200 gratings/mm
50 \mu m slit
With these settings, a 0.036 nm resolution was achieved in 7.



Some experiments like 1 used double monochromator setup for both pre- and post-sample spectral filtering.
Detection was done using Si based CCDs and Photodiodes. 2 Even used temperature-tuned Fabry-Perot etalons for sideband extraction in their microwave-modulated experiments - possibly entirely irrelevant to our current experiment.
Light Sources and Optical Configuration
Broadband light sources were most widely used, with LED sources centered at 580nm with 14nm width. 7 Describes the following setup:

The excitation light from a broadband white light source (Thorlabs SLS201L) was focused onto the sample with an objective lens with NA = 0.42 (Mitutoyo Plan Apo 20x)

Other (narrowband) sources I encountered are:

2 Frequency-doubled lasers at approximately 571nm
1 Tunable dye lasers

Optical path typically had focusing optics with the NA values previously mentioned for both sample illumination and light collection. Spot sizes achieved were ~ 3.1 \mu m FWHM. Aspheric lenses with higher NA were used to focus the beams to much smaller (~ 0.5 \mu m) diameter 4.
Specialized Optical Components
There were some setups like 4 that utilized 785-nm long-pass dichroic mirrors, 1000-nm short-pass filters, and bandpass filters centered at 580nm. Polarization control was achieved in 1 through linear polarizers that had specific orientations with respect to the crystal axes. (Noise eaters were used in the tunable dye laser setup, to reduce laser intensity fluctuations by a factor of 200. What are these?)
Critical Optical Parameters
Wavelength and Spectral Resolution
Yellow series Rydberg exciton detection requires excitation light to be in the range 571-580 nm. Higher spectral resolution is a must to observe higher-n states because of the close spacing.
Optical Power and Intensity
Powers ranged from 20 \mu W mm^{-2} 2 to 2 mW. Several studies used 50 \mu W for their temperature dependent measurements. I assume this is heavily dependent on the sample dimensions, though. Low power lasers (> 1 \mu W) were used to avoid depolarization effects 1. (It is assumed that depolarization here refers to a loss of coherence in the system. Maybe low power avoids driving the system into non-linear regimes)
Beam Geometry and Focusing
Focusing of the beam significantly impacted the detection performance of the experiments, as reported. Tight focusing was achieved with the NA values mentioned before. Spatial non-uniformity and broader excitation spot sizes where noted as factors that were detrimental to measurement quality.
Polarization Control
Useful for selective excitation of exciton states. Linear polarization with respect to crystal axes (i.e. [100], [010], [001], [110]) optimized the detection (why?). Excitation light polarization and angular structure strongly influenced the nonlinear optical response (why?).
Sample Requirements and Preparation
Crystal Quality and Structure
As stated before, natural crystals outperformed synthetic samples when compared on the basis of high-n state detection. (Do we have any X-Ray crystallographic data on our sample? All experiments reviewed had a single crystal).
Single crystals were preferred over polycrystalline samples to minimize strains. Cuts around crystal planes were done - but I am not entirely sure what that achieves.
Sample thickness and Geometry
Thickness varied from 30 \mu m to 700 n m depending on the preparation method. Natural crystals were mechanically polished. Synthetic films were much larger. The thickness of the sample is important - it must be measured accurately. This, and “wedge effects” influence interference phenomena, and must be considered during data analysis.
Purity and Defect Considerations
High purity and low defect density are of course essential for the detection of high-n states. Synthetic samples apparently show a significant excess of copper vacancies as compared to natural crystals. This affects spatial broadening, and spatial inhomogeneity.
Surface Quality and Mounting
Surface quality requirements included:

Smooth surface: Minimizes interference effects
Approximately flat and parallel (to beam path): Optimal transmission, lower reflective losses.
Often, CaF2 windows were often the substrate, put onto copper mounts.

Environmental and Experimental Conditions
Temperature Control
Cryogenic temperatures are universally required, as expected. 4K was common. Somewhat resolvable spectra was observed upto 150K.
Cooling
Here, we just use what is available.
Vibration Isolation and Shielding
Vibrations from the refrigerator should be avoided, and low-vibration setups are obviously preferred. Kapton spacers and Quartz plates are used to minimize mechanical stress.
The microwave modulated experiment required some thought regarding the antenna frequency responses of the setup.
Vacuum and Atmospheric Conditions
Vacuum environments were implied by the use of closed-cycle refrigerators and cryostats. Vacuums also reduce thermal noise significantly.
Detection Performance
The range of n-states that were observed is described before. Note that the linewidth broadening was state-dependent, with a FWHM of 14 GHz observed for 8p states. Linewidths may deviate from the expected scaling in some synthetic samples due to interactions with the background electron-hole plasma.
p- excitons have a much higher (2 orders of magnitude) intensities as compared to other spectral lines. Excitons and polariton states are distinguishable given good enough signal strength and resolution. Did not find too much on optimizing SNR.
Recommended Optimizations from survey

Spectral resolution is important - push it as far as possible without trading SNR.
Polarization control is useful to have. It can help distinguish between rydberg exciton states.
Temperature control should be good, to help with temperature-resolution and figure out how the spectra depends on it.
Low power excition lowers depolarization effects, and exciton-exciton interactions. but this reduces signal strength. This must be optimized (is there a straightforward way to do this?)
Thinner samples have enhanced interference effects.
Coherent transfer matrix method is a much more accurate analysis as compared to just Beer’s Law, especially for high-n states.
Elliot’s model was used to fit temperature dependent data and optimize detection parameters. (Look into how this was done)
Coherence of beam is important, and should be on the scale of the experiment.
Noise eaters can help, if there are intensity fluctuations from the excitation optics for some reason.

Statistical-Mechanics/Fields/Mean-Field-.title: Mean-Field Equation of State for the Ising Model
Statistical-Mechanics/Fields/Mean-Field-.content:
Here, we will be deriving the mean-field equation of state for the Ising Model using the self-consistency approach.
The Ising Model
The most famous model in existence, right after the Harmonic Oscillator perhaps.
In this model, we imagine a lattice of “spins” s_{i} that can (in the simplest version of the model) take one of two values - “Up” (+1) or “Down” (-1). The Hamiltonian for the system is given by:
H = - J \sum_{<i,j>} s_{i}s_{j} - h \sum_{i} s_{i}

The first term describes the nearest-neighbour interaction between the spins. J is a coupling constant that sets the energy scale for this particular interaction. When J > 0, we say that the system is ferromagnetic, and in the opposite case we say that the system is antiferromagnetic. <i,j> is shorthand notation that means “fix some i, then sum over all js that label the nearest neighbours of this i. Rinse and repeat for all such nearest neighbour pairs”.
h is the external field that the system is subject to. As we can see from the term, the field couples to each spin individually.

We of course also introduce a Temperature (T), which acts through the Boltzmann factor e^{ -E/k_{B}T } to introduce thermal fluctuations that favour disorder in the system.
The model seems simple enough, but the act of calculating the (canonical) partition function as given by:
Z = \sum_{\{s_{i}\}} e^{ -\beta E }
Where \{s_{i}\} denotes the sum over all configurations of the system, is generally impossible for large enough systems. Why? Because for N spins, the number of configurations is 2^N, and this sum quickly becomes very unwieldy in more than one dimensions. Thus, the interaction term is (obviously) what makes this problem so difficult.
The Mean-Field Approximation
To solve this problem, we first attempt to simplify it slightly. We replaced all of the detailed, fluctuating interactions that any single spin feels from its neighbours with a single, average or mean field.


                  
                  When is such an approximation justified? 
                  
                

While the precise demarcation of domains in which mean-field approximation works requires some involved discussion, we can roughly say this - The approximation is justifiable in systems where each spin interacts with a very large number of other spins (this happens in lattices where there are more “vertices” or on higher dimensions), as the law of large numbers suggests that the fluctuations around the average will be small.


Now that we have the idea set up for us, we can proceed to the derivation of the equation of state. We do this in two ways.
Derivation 1: Effective Field Approach
We proceed step by step:

First, isolate an arbitrary spin in the system. The interaction energy for this spin is given by

E_{i} = - s_{i} \left(  h + J \sum_{j \text{ near } i} s_{j} \right)

Make the approximation, where we define m = \langle s_{j} \rangle as the average magnetisation of the system. Since this system has translational invariance, all the spins experience this field on average.
Now, the effective Hamiltonian of the system becomes

H_{eff} (s_{i}) = -s_{i} (h + Jzm) = -s_{i} h_{eff}
Here, z is the coordination number of the system, or the number of nearest neighbours that any given spin has. We can see that we have converted out Hamiltonian into one that looks like a single spin interacting with an effective external field.
4. We can go ahead and calculate the average spin
\langle s_{i} \rangle = \frac{\left( \sum_{s_{i} \in \{-1,+1\}} s_{i} e^{ -\beta s_{i} h_{eff}}\right)}{\sum_{s_{i} \in \{-1,+1\}} e^{ -\beta s_{i h_{eff}} }} = \tanh(\beta h_{eff}) 

As our final step, we note that the average of the spin that we have focused on (i.e. s_{i}) must have an average that is equal to the average magnetisation of the lattice. This means. \langle s_{i} \rangle = m. Putting this in, we get a transcendental equation:

m = \tanh(\beta(h + zJm))
This is our mean-field equation of state. This can be solved iteratively to arrive at values of magnetisation of the system.
Derivation 2: Free Energy Approach
This alternative arrives at the same result as Derivation 1 does by approximating the system’s free energy and finding the magnetisation that minimizes it. This method is more extendable, as it connects more clearly to thermodynamics and also lays the groundwork for Landau Theory.
The Helmholtz Free Energy is:
F = E - TS
In our approximation,

We an estimate the energy E of a state of the system with uniform magnetisation m by replacing all the spins with a spin of value m. This leads us to

E(m) \approx - \frac{1}{2} H z J m^2 - Nhm

The entropy S of the system can be directly calculated from the combinatorial entropy of putting in enough up and down spins to create a net average magnetisation of m.

Statistical-Mechanics/Shannon-Entropy-an.title: Shannon Entropy and Maximum Entropy Principle
Statistical-Mechanics/Shannon-Entropy-an.content:
TL;DR 
                  
                

This note focuses on the information-theoretic and probabilistic underpinnings of statistical mechanics. We will be mostly concerned with the question:
On what basis do we assign probabilities to the microscopic states of a system?


Quantifying Uncertainty: Shannon Entropy
The concept of “entropy” in statistical mechanics is fundamentally related to the idea of “missing information” from information theory. Claude Shannon (They named the AI after him) figured out a way to quantify this missing information in a mathematically precise way.
We start with the intuitive idea of how “surprising” an event is. If an event is very likely, we are not surprised when it occurs. If it is very unlikely, we are surprised when it occurs. Shannon proposed that a reasonable measure for the “surprise” of an event (say, s(p)) for an event with a probability p should satisfy some simple axioms:


                  
                  Shannon's Axioms for surprise 
                  
                


It should be a continuous function of the probability p.
s(A) depends continuously on \mathbb{P}(X \in A)
It should be a decreasing function of p - this translates to the fact that a lower probability means a higher surprise.
s(A) is decreasing in \mathbb{P}(X \in A)
For two independent events, the total surprise should be a sum of their individual surprises.
s(A \cap B) = s(A) + s(B) if A and B are independent.





                  
                  Why were these axioms assumed? 
                  
                

While Axiom 2 is obvious, the other two are not very obvious.

 Please find out ➕ 2026-03-30



With these axioms, we can show that the functional form of s(p) is uniquely fixed.
Fixing the functional form of surprise
We require a function s: \text{Events} \to \mathbb{R} to describe “surprise”.
It happens that the only functional form that satisfies the Axioms stated above is:
s(A) = - \log(\mathbb{P}(X \in A))
Which means, that is the negative log of the probability of the event.
This result can be shown rigorously using Cauchy’s Functional equation. A reference for the derivation is given here. It adds an axiom, but the spirit of the derivation is the same.
Shannon then proceeds to define entropy from this:


                  
                  Shannon Entropy 
                  
                

Shannon Entropy is defined as the average surprise over all possible outcomes of a random variable.
If a random variable X can take on values \{x_{i}\} with corresponding probabilities \{p_{i}\}, then its entropy H(X) is given by:
H(X) = \langle s(p_{i}) \rangle = \sum_{i} p_{i}s(p_{i}) = - \sum_{i} p_{i} \log(p_{i}) 


The base of the logarithm used here is a matter or convention.

In Information Theory, base 2 is common because the idea of the “bit” is common.
In physics, the natural logarithm with base e is used, and the entropy is scaled by the Boltzmann factor k_{B} for dimensional consistency.

Thus, we can arrive directly at the definition of Gibbs entropy:
S = - k_{B} \sum_{i} p_{i} \ln(p_{i})
This quantity is maximized when our uncertainty is the greatest. This happens when the distribution is uniform, i.e. p_{i} = p_{j} \forall i, j.
The quantity is zero when there is no uncertainty - i.e. when only one p_{i} = 1 and everything else has a probability of 0.


                  
                  Biased Coin 
                  
                

X = \{H, T\} is the sample space of the coin toss.
\mathbb{P}(X = H) = p, where p is not necessarily \frac{1}{2}.
Then, the entropy is:
H(X) = -p\log(p) - (1-p)\log(1-p)
What does the plot look like?

Symmetric, and is zero at the two extremes - as one would expect. The highest average surprise is thus highest when everything is most random, i.e. in the case of the unbiased coin where p = \frac{1}{2}




                  
                  Jensen's Inequality 
                  
                

At this point, you might want to remind yourself of Jensen’s Inequality, as that is required to show that Entropy is maximized when the probability distribution is uniform.


The Principle of Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt)
Now that we can quantify missing information, we come back to the original problem.
Suppose that we have a physical system (make it simple, and assume a box with some gas in it). The system can be in one of a vast number of microstates i.


                  
                  Microstate 
                  
                

A state of the system defined by a specification of all the microscopic details, i.e. a point in the phase space of the system.


Typically, we do not know the exact microstate that the system resides in at any given moment. Instead, we have access to a few macroscopic properties which are averages over the microstates of the system.
As an example, the average energy of the system may be known:
\langle E \rangle = \sum_{i}p_{i}E_{i} = U
Where p_{i} is the probability of the system being in the microstate i and E_{i} is the energy of that microstate.


                  
                  Principle of Maximum Entropy 
                  
                

We should use a probability distribution \{p_{i}\} that maximizes the Gibbs entropy S of the system, subject to the constraints of our knowledge of the system through the macroscopic properties that we can measure.


This is obviously the right choice to make - any other choice of a probability distribution implies that we have more information than we actually have access to! By maximizing the entropy, we essentially are choosing a distribution that is maximally non-committal about the information that we do not have. It is the most honest thing to do.
How do we find such a distribution? We use the method of Lagrange Multipliers. This normally manifests as optimizing S[p_{i}] subject to the constraints of normalization \sum p_{i} = 1 and constraints of known macroscopic measurements like \sum p_{i} E_{i} = U.

Thermodynamics/Thermodynamics-(Home).content:
What is Thermodynamics?
Thermodynamics is the study of the restrictions on the possible properties of matter that follow from the symmetry properties of the fundamental laws of physics.
When we attempt to describe matter, we generally specify some easily quantifiable (i.e. something that may be compared to a standard easily) macroscopic quantity. This may be:

Mass/Weight
Position
Velocity/Momentum
And so on.

If we can decompose the system that we are describing into a collection of particles, then, classically speaking, a full characterization of the system can be achieved if we can simply state \{\vec{x},\vec{p}\} for all the particles in the system. Using the laws of mechanics, everything else can be found out from this. For more complicated systems, we might require an effective description of their internal states as well.
So why do the macroscopic quantities that we so commonly use work as a good substitute for this detailed description of the system? This is due to two fundamental characteristics of macroscopic measurement:

Extremely slow: Macroscopic measurements are extremely slow if compared to the timescales of atoms (or equivalent particles).
Extremely coarse: Macroscopic measurements are by definition very “coarse”, i.e they are carried out on lengthscales that are much larger than the atomic/particulate lengthscales.

These measurements simply can not extract the full details of the system - they instead give us a value that is averaged over both length and time!
Macroscopic observables are thus represented by the few quantities that can be derived from the full microscopic details of the system which also happen to be time and space independent on the scales of macroscopic measurement. Thermodynamics is concerned with the study of such quantities.
”Hidden” Modes
Of course, from the previous discussion it is clear that the system may also have modes that we can not resolve using macroscopic measurement. These modes may also carry energy, and thus must be accounted for to have a meaningful analysis of the behaviour of the system.


                  
                  Qualitative description: Heat 
                  
                

A transfer of energy via these “hidden” modes of the system is called heat.


Simple Systems
In all preliminary analysis, it will be assumed that these thermodynamic systems are simple - either purely mechanical or electromagnetic. This is entirely in the interests of exposition, and does not demonstrate a bound on the applicability of thermodynamics. Consider a simple system to be a system which satisfies the following properties:

Macroscopically homogenous
Isotropic
Uncharged
Large enough to neglect surface effects
Not acted upon by gravitational or electromagnetic field

Conservation Principles


                  
                  Conservation of energy 
                  
                

Energy is conserved - this is equivalent to the statement “The laws of physics are independent of time”.


Based on the conservation of energy, we are led to believe that macroscopic systems also have definite and precise energies. These energies must thus also be subject to the conservation principle.
Since we can only define the energy of a system with respect to a certain state (i.e. the value of energy of a system is always relative), we always first define a fiducial state and define it to have zero energy.
Equilibrium States
In all systems, there is a tendency to evolve towards states in which the properties of the system are defined by the intrinsic factors of the system, and not by the previously applied external influences on the state.


                  
                  Equilibrium States 
                  
                

These simple, terminal states which are by definition independent, where the properties of the system are determined solely by the intrinsic factors of the system are called equilibrium states.


First Postulate
Let us start with the first postulate for thermodynamics,

Unclassified/Defining-Evolution.title: Defining Evolution
Unclassified/Defining-Evolution.content:
TL;DR 
                  
                

This chapter will deal with the definitions of the three basic processes that make up evolutionary dynamics: replication, selection, and mutation. All “living” organisms (in the traditional sense) are modified continuously by these processes regardless of their details.


Evolution is a phenomena that shows up only in populations of living organisms. It goes something like this:

In the correct circumstances, living organisms replicate themselves - this might look like anything starting from binary fission in bacteria or virus replication in a host, to human birth. The genetic information that defines the organism is thus also passed on to the next generation.
In any population of organisms, there is competition between them. Due to this competition (and pressures due to other things such as organisms of another species predating on them or fighting for resources), there is a selection of particular individuals with specific qualities that allow them to out-compete the other organisms in the population. Selection thus prefers the survival (and thus, replication) of particular individuals over others, and thus we can conclude that it works against the creation of biological diversity.
The process of replication is not perfect. Sometimes, mistakes are made in the copying of genetic information from one generation to the next. This results in mutations, which (might) confer qualities to the next generation that were not present in the previous. Mutation is the cause for the biological diversity that we see.

Reproduction
Let us start with a simple test case: A bacterial cell, in a perfect environment (i.e. it has access to limitless resources), that undergoes binary division periodically every n minutes.


                  
                  World record bacterial growth rate 
                  
                

According to 1 the world record for such bacterial growth rates in binary division is n=20 \text{ mins}.


Of course, the population of bacteria after each division doubles. After t generations, we expect there to be 2^t bacterial. Note that at the world record rate mentioned above, 216 generations of growth like this would result in so many bacteria that their mass would be greater than that of the Earth. This is obviously non-physical, and we have to put in some physical constraints on the equation, which we will get to later. For now, we can write the growth law for this setup as a recursive relation:
\begin{equation}\label{eq\text{1}}
x_{t+1} = 2 x_{t}
\end{equation}
Here, x_{t} is the number of cells at some time t. Let x_{t=0} = x_{0}. Then the solution to this recursive equation is given by:
x_{t} = x_{0} 2^t

index.title: Welcome to CasioNotes!
index.content:
Hello, and welcome to my collection of (academic) notes!
This will be populated by my personal notes on Physics, Mathematics, Philosophy, and anything else that I deem to be “academic” enough :)
The website is organized as a wiki - so things are often not structured in a strict fashion. Please search for things that interest you, or simply browse through the folders, and let the links take you where they will.
A few words on the features of the site

The background is a simulation of a soft lattice with phonons propagating through it! The mouse “plucks” the lattice as it moves along. The lattice is also subject to thermal noise.
There is a dark mode, and a light one too for the psychopaths out there.
There is a reading mode, which turns off all the distracting things on the screen. This includes the simulation in the background.
I have a bionic reading mode! This is supposed to help you read faster, I think.
The site, be default, has a “Tufte-Style” footnote/citation display. This that footnotes and citations appear to the right of the text where they are referenced. This can be switched off.
That’s about it - you can access toggles for some of these things (including a global contrast increase and font size control) in the settings dialog box in the bottom right corner.

Thanks for visiing!
